Understanding the challenges of robotic-assisted surgery adoption: Perspectives from stakeholders and the general population on human-interaction, built environment, and training
==================================================================================================================================================================================

Author links open overlay panel Patrick Fuller a, Sara Kennedy b, Matthew Ball a, Holden Duffie a, Melanie Gainey c, Qi Luo a, Anjali Joseph b, Alfredo Carbonell c d, Jackie S.Cha a

Show more

Outline

Add to Mendeley

Share

Cite

[https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apergo.2024.104403](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apergo.2024.104403 "Persistent link using digital object identifier")[Get rights and content](https://s100.copyright.com/AppDispatchServlet?publisherName=ELS&contentID=S0003687024001807&orderBeanReset=true)

Full text access

Abstract
--------

This study aims to explore adoption barriers from three aspects critical to facilitating robotic-assisted-surgery (RAS) adoption: the human-robot-interaction, built-environment, and training. Guidelines for research and design are identified from these perspectives. This study consisted of three phases: 1) surveys to RAS stakeholders and a crowd-sourcing survey; 2) stakeholder focus groups; and 3) a workshop with subject-matter experts to prioritize future research for RAS. Barriers from the human-robot-interaction perspective include hesitancy among clinicians to trust automation and physical discomfort during prolonged RAS. Barriers impeding communication and workflow disruptions were identified considering the built-environment. Training barriers were exemplified by varying curriculums from formal (courses) to informal (peer training) for surgeons. Crowd-sourced survey results suggest socio-economic status plays a role in RAS perception. Design guidelines include: 1) improved ergonomics; 2) deliberate introduction of automation; 3) sufficient in-room storage for prospective operating rooms; and 4) the development of compact RAS devices for older/smaller rooms.

*   [Previous article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0003687024001789)
*   [Next article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0003687024001790)

Keywords
--------

Robotic surgery

Surgical nurses

Surgical technicians

1. Introduction
---------------

There has been a rapid rise in both the development and adoption of robotic-assisted surgery (RAS) in the United States and globally ([Rizzo et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0003687024001807#bib59); [Sheetz et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0003687024001807#bib64)). This rapid rise in adoption is best exemplified by prostate procedures in urology, which are now predominantly (more than 75%) completed robotically. Furthermore, new RAS systems have been developed or approved for different indications of use such as for surgical procedures in neurology, thoracic, and otolaryngology ([Latif and Park, 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0003687024001807#bib41); [Singh et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0003687024001807#bib66); [Tamaki et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0003687024001807#bib73)). By the end of 2022, over 7500 platforms have been installed, and the number of robotic surgeries conducted with Intuitive da Vinci robots exceeded 11 million globally ([Marchegiani et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0003687024001807#bib46)). This rise in the number of procedures and wider adoption of RAS has resulted in a market size of $7.1 billion in the United States as of 2023, with a projected market value of $26.4 billion by 2032 ([Surgical Robots Market, 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0003687024001807#bib72)).

RAS is a form of minimally invasive surgery in which surgeons perform surgery with the assistance of computer and software technology to control surgical instruments ([_Computer-Assisted Surgical Systems, 2022_](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0003687024001807#bib17)). The technology is subject to regulations by the Food and Drug Administration (FDA), and there have been several soft-tissue surgical robotic platforms introduced into the market ([Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0003687024001807#fig1)). These RAS devices and systems often include several components, including (1) the surgeon console, where the surgeon would sit during surgery to control surgical instruments and view the surgical field through a 3-Dimensional (3D) endoscope; (2) a patient/bedside cart that includes hinged mechanical arms that contain surgical instruments and an endoscope; and (3) a separate/visualization cart that will contain supporting hardware and software components ([_Computer-Assisted Surgical Systems, 2022_](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0003687024001807#bib17)).

![Image 9: Fig. 1](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S0003687024001807-gr1.jpg)
1.   [Download: Download high-res image (560KB)](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S0003687024001807-gr1_lrg.jpg "Download high-res image (560KB)")
2.   [Download: Download full-size image](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S0003687024001807-gr1.jpg "Download full-size image")

Fig. 1. Timeline of FDA Approvals and market releases of RAS platforms.

Despite the wider adoption of RAS platforms into operating rooms (OR), challenges still occur in adopting and integrating such complex devices. For example, communication challenges between surgeons and the surgical team have been identified due to the spatial constraints (e.g., OR size and layout) which negatively impact surgical workflows ([Catchpole et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0003687024001807#bib8); [Cohen et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0003687024001807#bib16)). Surgical human-robot interaction challenges also arise when observing the placement of the surgeon's console and its impact on traditional operative auditory and visual exchanges and non-verbal communications with other surgical team members ([Clanahan and Awad, 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0003687024001807#bib15); [Tiferes et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0003687024001807#bib74)). These human-robot interaction challenges have been further exasperated by the lack of standardized training programs and certifications ([Suh et al., 2010](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0003687024001807#bib71)). In addition, a recent systematic review of RAS training and credentialing indicates that there are currently a variety manual and automatic evaluative tools for RAS, and there is a need for procedure specific, standardized, and competency-based systems that deviate from the current use of case volume for competency ([Chen et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0003687024001807#bib13)). Other systematic reviews of RAS training also indicate that there are additional known barriers to address in terms of creating a curriculum that leverages formally organized system training and procedural training, and that issues such as longer operative times, modalities of training, and proficiency are still prevalent in the adoption of RAS platforms ([Schreuder et al., 2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0003687024001807#bib62); [Turchetti et al., 2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0003687024001807#bib76)). Outside of training, consensus is forming among RAS literature that these platforms are typically not cost effective due to their high costs and longer operative times ([Reddy et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0003687024001807#bib58); [Turchetti et al., 2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0003687024001807#bib76)). To improve surgeon performance and enhance safety, which could subsequently decrease operative times, efforts are being made to adopt elements of image guidance, augmented reality, and automation ([Diana and Marescaux, 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0003687024001807#bib21); [Han et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0003687024001807#bib26)), which consequently creates a necessary tradeoff between technological advancements and costs for adoption.

To understand how to integrate RAS platforms into surgical workflows on a system-level, there is a need to investigate RAS adoption factors systemically to establish a foundation for the future integration of surgical robotic-assisted technology. Previous work has identified barriers in RAS focusing on organizational perspectives, such as work by [Lawrie et al., 2022a](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0003687024001807#bib43), [Lawrie et al., 2022b](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0003687024001807#bib44), which explored barriers across three stages of implementation for National Health Service hospitals in the United Kingdom. In addition, there have also been efforts by a colloquium of multidisciplinary researchers to propose future directions for the research and development of future RAS devices from device, clinician, system, and patient perspectives in various design stages ([Marcus et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0003687024001807#bib47)). While these studies identify barriers and develop frameworks for the future development of RAS devices, additional barriers to RAS adoption may be identified from a more holistic perspective that incorporates specific considerations of the human-robot interactions, the built environment, and RAS training in not only initial device implementation, but also its integration into existing clinical infrastructures to consider both the prospective and retrospective integration of RAS. Thus, this multi-stage study aims to explore the barriers and enablers impacting the public and key RAS stakeholders within the context of the American healthcare system from this broader perspective.

Human factors and ergonomics (HFE) approaches can be utilized to systemically analyze RAS integration to address the dynamics and complexities of hospital systems and departments nested within larger health care systems ([Dekker and Leveson, 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0003687024001807#bib19)). By focusing on how humans are interacting with technology and others in the complex sociotechnical system, this work differs from the work done by [Lawrie et al., 2022a](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0003687024001807#bib43), [Lawrie et al., 2022b](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0003687024001807#bib44), who utilized the Theoretical Domains Framework and Consolidated Framework for implementation Research and the work done by [Marcus et al. (2024)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0003687024001807#bib47) that systematically evaluated and outlined recommendations to guide RAS stakeholders through all stages of evaluation in the Idea, Development, Exploration, Assessment and Long-term monitoring (IDEAL). Our work is more in line with the methodology employed by [Høyland et al. (2023)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0003687024001807#bib29), who identified barriers and advantages to the implementation of RAS through 13 in-depth interviews with managers and sharp-end personnel at a regional hospital in Norway while utilizing the System Engineering Initiative for Patient Safety (SEIPS) framework. This HFE approach utilized by Høyland and colleages is further corroborated by previous literature outside of RAS that has utilized the SEIPS model during a crisis to identify challenges in transitioning to telemedicine and organizational challenges impacting physiotherapy work structure as a result of the COVID-19 pandemic ([Wooldridge et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0003687024001807#bib83)) and in evaluating a sociotechnical system before and after implementing an intervention for improving opioid prescribing ([Montague et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0003687024001807#bib51)). Similarly, this work will also adopt the SEIPS framework to expand on the typical micro-ergonomic and operating room focused studies of the current RAS literature ([Xie and Carayon, 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0003687024001807#bib84)) while considering mesoergonomic factors ([Karsh et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0003687024001807#bib36)). Instead of utilizing the SEIPS model to identify barriers, this work will utilize the SEIPS 2.0 model due to its incorporation of an “external environment” feature that incorporates macro-level factors outside of an organization that is critical for the HFE approach ([Holden et al., 2013](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0003687024001807#bib28)). It is important to note that, while this work will explore RAS adoption from the surgical human robotic interaction (people, tools & technology), built environment (internal and external environment), and RAS training (people), these components largely tie into the SEIPS framework and focus primarily on work system factors and will be used interchangeably. Stakeholder surveys for clinicians and the public will also be utilized to further understand the external environment and its potential impact on organizations that utilize RAS.

### 1.1. Surgical human-robot interaction (people, tools & technology)

The surgical human-robot interaction (HRI) describes the interactions between the human surgical team and the robotic system. The surgical team is defined as a group of any medical personnel that conducts both proximate and remote interactions with surgical robots during an RAS procedure. The surgical robot refers to any RAS devices or systems that are used during an active RAS procedure. Surgical HRI involves communication – information transfer – and physical interactions between humans and robots ([Abdelaal et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0003687024001807#bib1)). Within the surgical HRI, research in RAS primarily focuses on the surgeon's interaction with significantly less literature on the surgical team (e.g., nurses, technicians, anesthesiologists) ([Sgarbura and Vasilescu, 2010](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0003687024001807#bib63)). To identify the current challenges related to human-robot teams, team and robot interactions will be explored holistically so that they can be considered for future robot design.

### 1.2. Built environment (internal and external environment)

The built environment refers to the surroundings and space where people live and work, and in the case of RAS includes elements of the OR environment. Studies show that the built environment impacts workflow, which is defined as the set of tasks and processes for patient care, equipment, instrument, and supply flows in and out of the OR. Often, RAS systems are integrated into existing ORs that are not designed to support the equipment and worker needs for these types of surgeries. The size and layout of ORs are significant constraints that impact the effective use of this technology as well as surgical workflows ([Catchpole et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0003687024001807#bib8)). Several studies have shown that ORs are highly cluttered and crowded, leading to frequent disruptions during surgery ([Alarcon and Berguer, 1996](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0003687024001807#bib3); [Brogmus et al., 2007](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0003687024001807#bib6); [Fraind et al., 2002](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0003687024001807#bib23); [Joseph et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0003687024001807#bib35); [Palmer et al., 2013](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0003687024001807#bib53)). This is a significant challenge in RAS due to the presence of large robotic equipment and the associated clutter that can impact patient safety ([Ahmad et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0003687024001807#bib2); [Catchpole et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0003687024001807#bib10)). Although there are structural and design guidelines for designing ORs ([Guidelines for Design and Construction of Outpatient Facilities, 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0003687024001807#bib25)), these are minimum guidelines and fail to address the spatial, workflow, and team communication considerations for RAS. Since RAS workflows are different compared to laparoscopic and open surgery, it is important to evaluate OR design guidelines in the context of the dynamic interactions between people, equipment and technology, and tasks that are specific to RAS. As new RAS technologies are developed, there is a need to consider how existing and future physical spaces should be adapted and designed to support the integration of this technology into new and existing ORs.

### 1.3. RAS training (people)

With the rapid adoption of RAS across surgical specialties, training programs have been developed specific to operations (e.g., assessment of technical skills) and hospital credentialing and vary across the United States ([Huffman et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0003687024001807#bib31); [Pradarelli et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0003687024001807#bib57); [Stefanidis et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0003687024001807#bib70)). Current RAS training ranges from bench or lab instruction to virtual simulation ([Imai et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0003687024001807#bib32)). RAS training is also unique, as trainees are not only residents and fellows, but also surgeons that may be experts in laparoscopic and open procedures but are novices in RAS ([Chahal et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0003687024001807#bib12)). Moreover, many hospitals have their own methods for training their staff or rely on pre-established training programs created by RAS manufacturers. Several studies have shown that the use of a combination of these different styles (virtual and physical) of training can lead to proficient skill improvements for those undergoing RAS training ([Butterworth et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0003687024001807#bib7); [Chahal et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0003687024001807#bib11)). However, there is still a need for standardized RAS training on the technology used to facilitate efficient methods of tracking, teaching, and improving surgical skills for RAS operators (e.g., surgeons, residents, and fellows) and for surgical staff (e.g., nurses and technicians) ([Smith et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0003687024001807#bib69)).

2. Methods
----------

This multi-phase study consisted of three phases: 1) surveys to understand RAS stakeholders' perspectives of RAS and a crowd-sourcing survey to capture the perception of prospective and past patients of RAS on how RAS compares to open or laparoscopic surgery; 2) focus groups and interviews to gain insights into the worker's experience with RAS; and 3) a workshop with subject matter experts to discuss findings and prioritize future research for RAS. This research was approved by the university and healthcare system Institutional Review Boards.

### 2.1. Study 1: Surveys

#### 2.1.1. RAS Stakeholder survey

Stakeholders of RAS were identified as clinicians and their trainees (e.g., surgeons, nurses, anesthesiologist, technicians), engineers, architects, hospital administrators, manufacturers, regulators, and researchers. A survey was developed to capture perceptions of experiences with the surgical HRI, built environment, and training curriculum from individuals representing a range of different roles, experience levels, and areas of expertise. Respondents were asked to answer specific questions based on their primary role (e.g., Surgeon, Researcher, Anesthesiologist). The survey was disseminated from February 2023 to July 2023 via engineering and clinical academic listservs (e.g., Human Factors and Ergonomics Society [HFES], Institute of Industrial and Systems Engineers [IISE], Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers Robotics and Automation Society [IEEE-RAS], and subgroups of the Society of American Gastrointestinal and Endoscopic Surgeons [SAGES] and the Association of Perioperative Registered Nurses [AORN]) and word of mouth by the research team. Participants were not compensated for their time. The RAS stakeholder survey can be found in [Appendix A](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0003687024001807#appsec1). Major built environment issues from the survey were contextualized within five phases of RAS ([Catchpole et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0003687024001807#bib9)).

#### 2.1.2. Crowdsourcing survey

To capture the public perception of how individuals felt about RAS, as opposed to open or laparoscopic surgery, a crowdsourcing survey was developed. To record this perception, participants were briefly informed on the concepts of laparoscopic surgery, open surgery, and RAS and were then prompted to rate their perception of comfortability with receiving RAS; how safe they felt with receiving RAS; and their perception of reliability for RAS among other questions. Answer choices for comfortability and safety corresponded to a five-point Likert scale, and reliability corresponded to a three-point Likert scale for analysis. The survey also included demographic questions, including previous experience with RAS. The survey was disseminated in May 2023 through Prolific ([www.prolific.co](http://www.prolific.co/), Oxford, United Kingdom). Participants had to be United States residents, over the age of 18, and English speaking. Participants were compensated at a rate of $25/hr., with the survey lasting an average of 2.8 min. The crowdsourcing survey can be found in [Appendix B](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0003687024001807#appsec1). Both surveys were developed and conducted via Qualtrics (Qualtrics, Utah, United States). Non-parametric analysis of variance (Kruskal-Wallis) and subsequent post hoc Dunn tests on survey results were completed in R Studio (Posit, Massachusetts, United States, version 2023.09.1+494). Demographic data was aggregated into larger groups that are consistent with United States Census groupings to increase group sample sizes. Distances from healthcare facility were aggregated to be above or below the average distance from healthcare facility for individuals in suburban areas ([Lam et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0003687024001807#bib40)). To counteract conflated type I error rate for multiple comparisons across demographics, the Bonferroni correction for p-values was utilized for all statistical tests ([Jafari and Ansari-Pour, 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0003687024001807#bib33)). Regression decision trees were modeled using the JMP statistical software (JMP, North Carolina, United States, version 17.2) to predict demographic influences on perception scores.

enriched_points.expanded_content: High initial costs of robotic surgery equipment deter hospitals from adoption, impacting budgets and limiting accessibility to advanced surgical technologies, as noted in recent studies.
Robotic surgery devices face stringent FDA regulations, demanding extensive clinical trial evidence, which can slow down the approval process and hinder timely integration into surgical practice.
Skepticism regarding robotic surgery's effectiveness persists among patients, influenced by varying experiences and a lack of understanding about the technology's safety and reliability.
Ongoing research is crucial to evaluate robotic surgery's cost-effectiveness and clinical benefits, addressing current gaps in knowledge and fostering trust among healthcare providers and patients.
